metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit AC2 sub-case: a request carrying the secret header with a *wrong* (present but incorrect) value is treated identically to absent (identity stripped, 401), directly exercising the constant-time compare.
findings.finding: The unit test AC for the shared-secret check specifies 'without the secret' but does not explicitly require a test for a *wrong* (present but invalid) secret value. For a constant-time-compare gate, these are distinct cases: an absent header vs. a header with a wrong value. Failing to explicitly cover the wrong-value case means the test suite could pass even if the constant-time compare is misconfigured (e.g., always-truthy on non-empty string).
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2 (unit test for middleware/verifier)
findings.suggested_fix: Extend AC2: 'with the secret header present but containing an incorrect value, the identity header is also stripped and the request returns 401 — confirming the constant-time compare rejects wrong values, not just absent ones.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify (and document in the plan) the specific FastMCP ≥1.9 API used to: (a) obtain an ASGI app object the middleware wraps, and (b) confirm that this HTTP path is active in production deployment (not just stdio); add a note explaining the stdio vs HTTP transport relationship so the security scope is clear.
findings.finding: The plan asserts 'The middleware runs before the MCP app' but does not verify or specify how an ASGI middleware is attached to a FastMCP server instance — the current codebase uses `FastMCP.run()` (stdio mode) and there is no established seam in the repo for wrapping FastMCP with ASGI middleware. The FastMCP HTTP ASGI surface attachment mechanism is unverified.
findings.location: What section: 'The middleware runs before the MCP app'; Integration test AC: 'httpx ASGITransport'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify the concrete state-passing contract between the ASGI middleware and the TokenVerifier-shaped component (e.g., 'middleware writes verified identity to `scope["proxy_principal"]`; verifier reads `scope.get("proxy_principal")`') so there is exactly one named channel and the integration test exercises the full call chain.
findings.finding: The plan describes a two-component design where the ASGI middleware writes a verified identity into a 'request-scoped principal' and the TokenVerifier-shaped component reads that identity later during composite evaluation, but nowhere specifies the concrete state-passing contract between the two components. The two plausible mechanisms — writing into the ASGI `scope` dict (e.g., `scope['proxy_principal']`) vs. a Python `contextvars.ContextVar` — have different semantics: ContextVar propagation depends on the async task context in which the middleware sets it, while scope-dict writes are visible to any component holding a reference to that scope. A mismatch (middleware sets one, verifier reads the other) or a ContextVar reset at an async boundary silently breaks the data flow without raising an exception, yielding a verifier that always returns None and falls through to 401.
findings.location: What section: 'extracts the identity header into a request-scoped principal'; verifier subsection: 'turns that verified request-scoped identity into an AccessToken'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to the What/Approach section: 'The middleware stores the verified principal in the ASGI `scope` dict under a private key (e.g., `_proxy_principal`); the verifier reads that same key. Using `scope` (not a ContextVar) is preferred because ASGI scope is owned by a single request, has no async-propagation subtleties, and the full middleware→verifier chain is exercised by the existing httpx ASGITransport integration tests.'

findings.finding: The plan asserts the proxy-guard ASGI middleware 'runs before the MCP app' to strip/repopulate headers before the S2 composite auth handler sees the request, but does not name the FastMCP API used to insert it. The specific hook point — whether to wrap FastMCP's underlying Starlette app, use a FastMCP-level middleware API, or wrap the ASGI callable returned by `mcp.streamable_http_app()` — is unspecified. If the middleware is inserted AFTER FastMCP's own OAuth bearer-auth handler (which is where the S2 composite runs), the strip-then-repopulate guarantee is lost: a spoofed `X-Forwarded-User` header would be evaluated by the composite before the guard can strip it.
findings.location: What section / Approach & rationale — 'The middleware runs before the MCP app'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Approach section, specify: 'The proxy-guard middleware wraps the top-level ASGI app (`server.asgi_app` or the equivalent FastMCP streamable-http callable) so it intercepts every request before FastMCP's own OAuth middleware layer.' Add an observable oracle to the integration AC: assert in the test that the middleware wraps at the outermost ASGI layer (e.g., the middleware's scope-mutating logic is observable when using httpx `ASGITransport` pointed at the wrapped app).

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an AC (unit or integration) that, when the shared secret IS valid, X-Forwarded-* headers from the proxy are NOT stripped and are visible to the downstream app (or explicitly reconsider and document if they should be stripped in both cases).
findings.finding: The Security section describes that X-Forwarded-* headers pass through to the downstream app when the shared secret IS valid ('strips the entire X-Forwarded-* family on its side unless the shared secret validates'), but no AC tests or specifies this pass-through behavior. This is a described deliverable with no covering criterion: an implementer could also-strip on valid secret and silently break real-world use cases (e.g. oauth2-proxy populating X-Forwarded-Email for app-layer use).
findings.location: Security section ('rebar additionally strips the entire X-Forwarded-* family on its side unless the shared secret validates') vs. Acceptance Criteria (AC 3 only tests the strip-on-invalid-secret path)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Acceptance Criteria: 'Unit test asserts that when the shared-secret header is valid, the X-Forwarded-* family (e.g. X-Forwarded-Email, X-Forwarded-Groups) present in the proxy request are NOT stripped and reach the downstream handler.' If the intended behavior is actually to strip them always and only inject via the request-scoped principal, update the Security section to say so explicitly and drop the 'unless' qualifier.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an ADR (or a dedicated section in CLAUDE.md / a security-runbook entry) capturing the trusted-proxy architectural decision: the strip-then-repopulate contract, the shared-secret model, the operator prerequisite (proxy must strip client-supplied identity headers), and the rationale — do not rely solely on S7's env-var reference regeneration to cover this.
findings.finding: The plan introduces a novel trusted-proxy authentication pattern — including a new ASGI middleware layer, a shared-secret/header-stripping security contract, and a specific operator prerequisite (proxy MUST overwrite client-supplied identity headers) — but names no ADR, CLAUDE.md update, or design-doc entry to capture this architectural decision durably. 'Docs regenerated by S7' refers only to env-var/config reference material, not a decision record.
findings.location: ## What / ## Security / Acceptance Criteria (first bullet, 'Docs regenerated by S7')
findings.suggested_fix: Create `docs/adr/005-trusted-proxy-auth.md` (or equivalent) that records: (1) the decision to use ASGI middleware + TokenVerifier-shaped composite participant, (2) the shared-secret + strip-then-repopulate security model, (3) the operator prerequisite verbatim, and (4) the CVE references motivating it. Reference this ADR from the S7 docs regeneration task.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that the middleware logs (at least at WARN level) each invalid-secret rejection (with source IP if available) and each header-strip event, and logs (at DEBUG/INFO) each successful proxy authentication, so operators can detect spoofing probes and audit proxy auth.
findings.finding: The plan specifies no observability instrumentation (logging, metrics, or alerting) for the new security-critical failure points introduced by the middleware: invalid-secret rejections (potential spoofing probes), stripped-header events (attack signal), and successful proxy authentications. Operators cannot detect an ongoing header-injection attack or audit proxy auth usage.
findings.location: Security section / Approach & rationale / AC (no AC covers logging or metrics)
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section and a new AC: 'The middleware MUST log a structured WARNING on every request where inbound X-Forwarded-* or identity headers are present but the secret is invalid or absent (potential spoofing probe), and log INFO on each successful proxy authentication (identity, scopes granted). Log entries MUST NOT include the raw secret value.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify that any exception raised inside the middleware's header-validation/stripping path MUST be caught and result in a fail-closed outcome: all X-Forwarded-* and identity headers are stripped and the request proceeds as unauthenticated (no identity set), with the exception logged at ERROR level.
findings.finding: The plan does not specify exception-handling behavior inside the ASGI middleware's header-stripping and secret-validation logic. If normalization or comparison raises an unexpected exception (e.g., malformed header encoding), the middleware's fallback behavior is undefined — it could propagate a 500 (leaking info) or, worse, allow the request through without stripping.
findings.location: What section — 'The middleware runs before the MCP app' / Security section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'The middleware MUST wrap its entire header-inspection and stripping logic in a try/except block; on any exception the middleware MUST strip all X-Forwarded-* and identity/secret headers, treat the request as unauthenticated, and log the exception at ERROR level before continuing — it MUST NOT propagate the exception upstream.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Specify (or link to) the exact S2-delivered interface contract — the concrete type name(s), field names (`client_id`, `scopes`, `resource`), and config key (`auth_resource_server_url`) — so S5 implementors can verify the contract against S2's actual output rather than assuming it.
findings.finding: The plan asserts specific structural contracts for the S2 seam — `TokenVerifier`-shaped component, `AccessToken` type with fields `client_id`, `scopes`, `resource`, and `auth_resource_server_url` config key — as implementation targets, but none of these symbols exist anywhere in the current codebase and the S2 ticket is unresolvable. If S2 delivers a different interface shape (different type name, field names, or config key), S5's implementation would be silently wrong.
findings.location: What section / Security section: 'a TokenVerifier-shaped component', 'mints an AccessToken with client_id=the identity, scopes=auth_proxy_scopes, and resource=auth_resource_server_url', 'passes the composite audience check exactly like a token verifier'
findings.suggested_fix: Add a 'Preconditions from S2' subsection listing: the exact Python type S5's verifier must return (e.g. `AccessToken` from `rebar._mcp_auth`), its required fields and types, and the exact config key name for the resource server URL. Reference the S2 AC that defines them.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Verify S1's non-loopback gate exists (or will exist when S5 is implemented) and cite its specific config key(s) so the 'no new bind key needed' exclusion is grounded rather than assumed.
findings.finding: The plan asserts S1 already delivers 'an existing non-loopback gate (both allowlists + TLS-at-edge ack)' that governs non-loopback binds for S5, but S1 is not resolvable as a ticket and no such gate exists in the current codebase.
findings.location: Security section: 'a non-loopback bind is governed by S1's existing non-loopback gate (both allowlists + TLS-at-edge ack) — no new bind key'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Replace the fabricated/unverifiable CVE identifiers ('CVE-2026-40575 / CVE-2025-64484') in the Approach section with real, verifiable references (e.g., actual oauth2-proxy CVEs, OWASP header-injection guidance, or the oauth2-proxy security documentation URL) that support the forwarded-header-spoofing mitigation rationale.
findings.finding: The Approach section cites 'oauth2-proxy CVE-2026-40575 / CVE-2025-64484' as documented mitigations for the forwarded-header-spoofing class, but CVE-2026-40575 carries a 2026 year-prefix (a future year not yet in the CVE calendar) and neither identifier can be verified — they appear to be hallucinated CVE numbers used to justify a security-critical design decision.
findings.location: Approach & rationale section: 'This is the documented mitigation for the forwarded-header-spoofing class (oauth2-proxy CVE-2026-40575 / CVE-2025-64484)'
findings.suggested_fix: Replace with a real reference such as: 'This is the documented mitigation for the forwarded-header-spoofing class (see oauth2-proxy security docs: https://oauth2-proxy.github.io/oauth2-proxy/configuration/tls and OWASP header injection guidance).' Remove the CVE citations entirely if no real CVEs apply.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add an explicit requirement (Security section + AC) that the proxy shared-secret header value MUST be redacted/suppressed from all request logs and traces; specify that the middleware must not pass the raw secret value to any logging call.
findings.finding: The plan does not require that the proxy shared-secret header value be redacted from ASGI access logs or debug-level request logging. If the MCP server runs with header-logging enabled (a common debug pattern), the shared secret travels in plaintext log lines — a plaintext-secret-on-boundary-path gap.
findings.location: Security section / middleware spec — no mention of log redaction
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the Security section: 'The middleware MUST NOT log the value of the shared-secret header; if ASGI access logging is enabled, the secret header must be replaced with a redacted placeholder (e.g., [REDACTED]) before the log record is written.' Add a corresponding AC: 'Unit test asserts that a request containing the shared-secret header does not produce a log record containing the secret value.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a Security section declaration for the proxy-to-rebar transport leg: state that non-loopback proxy-to-rebar traffic MUST use TLS (or an equivalent private-network control), and that the 'TLS-at-edge ack' in S1 covers only the client-facing bind, not this inter-service channel — either add a new transport requirement or explicitly document that the shared-secret pattern requires the proxy and rebar to co-reside on the same host/loopback.
findings.finding: The plan delegates all non-loopback transport security to S1's existing gate, but S1's gate governs the client-facing bind address — not the proxy-to-rebar channel. In a deployment where the edge proxy and the rebar backend run on separate hosts (a common cloud/Kubernetes pattern), the shared secret and identity headers traverse an inter-host network segment. The plan does not declare a transport security requirement (TLS, mTLS, or private-network guarantee) for this proxy-to-rebar leg, leaving a plaintext-secret-over-network gap undeclared.
findings.location: Security section — 'Loopback bind is the intended deployment (S1 default); a non-loopback bind is governed by S1's existing non-loopback gate'
findings.suggested_fix: In the Security section, add: 'When rebar and the fronting proxy run on separate hosts, the proxy-to-rebar channel MUST be TLS-protected (or the path MUST be a private-network segment with no cross-tenant reachability); the shared secret MUST NOT transit a plaintext inter-host connection. The S1 TLS-at-edge ack covers the client bind only — operators must separately attest the inter-service transport.' Optionally add an AC: 'Documentation (S7) explicitly calls out the proxy-to-rebar transport requirement for non-loopback deployments.'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Resolve the conflict between AC1 (manifest check passes in this story) and the 'Docs regenerated by S7' note: either (a) explicitly include a `server.json` stub entry for the new env-vars as part of this story's scope so the manifest check can pass immediately, OR (b) split AC1 — mark the `MCP_ENV_VARS`/`McpConfig` part as in-scope here and tag the server.json/manifest-test part with `[blocked-by S7]` so it is not a gate for this story's completion.
findings.finding: AC1 states that `scripts/check_server_manifest.py` passes and 'both files contain the new env-var names' as a completion criterion for THIS story, but the What section explicitly defers server.json population to S7 ('Docs regenerated by S7'). An executor cannot determine whether updating server.json is in-scope here or must wait for S7, and the manifest-drift test will fail if server.json is left for S7.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC1; What section — last sentence ('Docs regenerated by S7')
findings.suggested_fix: Add a sentence to AC1: 'This story adds a placeholder entry for each REBAR_MCP_AUTH_PROXY_* key to server.json so the manifest drift test passes immediately; S7 will regenerate the full doc block but must not remove these entries.' This removes the ambiguity without creating a double-write risk.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a test assertion for the case where the secret is valid but the identity header is absent or empty: the verifier should return None (resulting in 401), not mint a token with an empty client_id.
findings.finding: No acceptance criterion covers the case where the shared secret is valid but the identity header (`auth_proxy_identity_header`) is absent or empty. The verifier behavior in this case is unspecified — should it return None (fall through) or raise? Without a test, an implementation could inadvertently use an empty string as client_id or raise an uncaught exception.
findings.location: Acceptance Criteria — AC2, AC6; What section (verifier description)

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Clarify in the plan that `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py` is a **new file to create** (not a pre-existing file to modify), so the edit target description is accurate.
findings.finding: `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py` does not exist in the repository — the plan says 'Add a trusted-proxy mode to src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py' implying it pre-exists, but it is actually a new file that must be created.
findings.location: What section: 'Add a trusted-proxy mode to `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py`'
findings.suggested_fix: Change 'Add a trusted-proxy mode to `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py`' to 'Create `src/rebar/_mcp_auth.py`' in the What section.

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Enumerate `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` / `docs/mcp-reference.md` as a consumer of `MCP_ENV_VARS` that S5 must also update (or explicitly acknowledge it regenerates the doc as part of this story, not deferred to S7).
findings.finding: `scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` is an unenumerated consumer of `MCP_ENV_VARS`: it imports it directly and uses it to render `docs/mcp-reference.md`; a live CI drift gate (`test.yml` lines 308-314, `gerrit-verify.yaml` lines 262-268) diffs `docs/mcp-reference.md` after running the script and will fail in S5's own CI run if `MCP_ENV_VARS` is extended but the doc is not regenerated.
findings.location: What section / AC1: 'their derived REBAR_MCP_AUTH_PROXY_* env-var names go in MCP_ENV_VARS (src/rebar/mcp_server.py) + server.json; and scripts/check_server_manifest.py passes'
findings.suggested_fix: Add to AC1: 'also run `python scripts/gen_mcp_reference.py` and commit the updated `docs/mcp-reference.md` (the CI drift gate for that file runs in S5's own CI and will fail if stale).'

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Retrieve (or author) the parent epic ticket for the MCP auth story series (S1–S7) and confirm S5's scope — ASGI middleware for proxy header stripping, `auth_proxy_*` config fields, `TokenVerifier`-shaped proxy verifier, and startup fail-closed rule — is a faithful subset of the parent's What/Scope/ACs with no contradictions.
findings.finding: The parent epic ticket for S5 is not retrievable from the ticket system (every candidate ID returns 'Ticket not found'), so the G7 leaf-parent containment check cannot be completed — it is impossible to verify that S5's scope is a strict subset of, and does not contradict, the parent's plan.
findings.location: Plan preamble / Scope / Depends on S2

findings.checklist_item: - [ ] Add a brief rationale in the plan acknowledging the existing `_guard_ok`-style app-level guard pattern (opcert_service, review_bot) and explaining why a standalone ASGI middleware is preferred here (e.g., it must strip headers before they reach the FastMCP routing layer, which a per-endpoint guard cannot do).
findings.finding: The codebase already has two established implementations of the shared-secret header guard pattern (`src/rebar/opcert_service/app.py` with `X-Opcert-Guard` + `hmac.compare_digest`, and `src/rebar/review_bot/app.py` with `?token=` + `hmac.compare_digest`) that the plan does not acknowledge. The plan should confirm whether a standalone ASGI middleware is preferred over the codebase's existing app-level guard function pattern, and if so, why.
findings.location: What section: 'A tiny ASGI middleware is the right seam'; Approach & rationale section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to Approach & rationale: 'Unlike the existing per-endpoint guard-function pattern (opcert_service, review_bot), a pre-routing ASGI middleware is required here because the strip-on-invalid-secret behavior must prevent spoofed headers from reaching ANY FastMCP route — not just the authenticated endpoints. A per-endpoint guard cannot strip headers from the ASGI scope.'

findings.finding: The plan does not specify whether `auth_proxy_scopes` is required, optional with a default (e.g., empty set), or causes a startup failure when absent/empty. This is security-relevant: silently granting empty scopes vs. failing closed are meaningfully different behaviors, and an executing agent cannot choose between them without guessing.
findings.location: What / config fields description and Security section
findings.suggested_fix: Add to the config field description: 'auth_proxy_scopes: optional; defaults to empty set (no scopes granted); an empty value is valid and fail-safe — operators SHOULD set an explicit scope list. Startup does NOT fail on absence.' (or the opposite policy if fail-closed is intended, consistent with the secret-env-var behavior).

findings.finding: The `auth_proxy_scopes` config field has no specified optionality, default, or failure behavior. The plan is explicit that `auth_proxy_secret_env` must be present and non-empty at startup or the verifier refuses to start, but `auth_proxy_scopes` has no analogous rule. Since scopes gate what the proxy-authenticated principal can do, an absent or empty `auth_proxy_scopes` is security-relevant: does it grant no scopes (deny everything)? All scopes? Cause startup failure? This decision rule is absent.
findings.location: What section — `auth_proxy_scopes` field description / Security section

metrics.claim_path: no-llm/no-network (structural; the fast claim check is a local HMAC verify)
